steps.ordered: Ultrasound Abdomen (LIVER OR GALLBLADDER US (SINGLE ORGAN))

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary obstruction; passed common bile duct stone with residual biliary tree changes
ensemble.information_gap: Current real-time status of the gallbladder, biliary tree (CBD diameter, wall thickening, stones), and pancreas at THIS institution — the outside imaging is from different dates and facilities, and the most recent MRCP showed biliary irregularities of uncertain etiology with a recommendation for follow-up; the physician needs a fresh baseline sonographic assessment upon transfer
ensemble.expected_finding: Gallbladder wall thickening, pericholecystic fluid, cholelithiasis, or a dilated common bile duct suggesting ongoing cholecystitis or choledocholithiasis; alternatively, interval improvement confirming a passed stone
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: The patient transferred with outside imaging showing CBD dilation (7.6 mm), gallbladder wall thickening, and biliary tree irregularities on MRCP, but no same-institution imaging exists; a repeat RUQ ultrasound is the appropriate first-line, radiation-free, pregnancy-safe study to establish a current baseline and guide management decisions.
grounding: History: 'presents to labor and delivery as a transfer from ___ with cholelithiasis and pancreatitis'
Outside RUQ U/S: 'Gallbladder wall thickening and edema with common bile duct dilatation and positive sonographic ___ sign'
Outside RUQ U/S: 'common bile duct dilated measuring 7.6mm'
Outside MRCP: 'A few areas of narrowing and dilatation of the intra- and extra-hepatic biliary tree'
Outside MRCP: 'No evidence of choledocholithiasis' and 'No MR evidence of pancreatitis'
Outside MRCP: 'Edematous nondistended gallbladder with small amount of sludge/tiny stones'
Labs: 'Lipase: 69.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)' — mildly elevated, trending down from 488 at outside facility
Labs: 'Bilirubin, Total: 0.8 mg/dL' — normalized from T.bili 2.2 at outside facility
History: 'G1P0 at ___' — pregnancy status makes radiation-free imaging preferable
ensemble.reasoning: This pregnant patient transferred with documented cholelithiasis, prior CBD dilation, gallbladder wall thickening, and biliary tree irregularities on outside imaging, but no current-institution imaging exists. Although her labs are trending toward normal (lipase 69, total bilirubin 0.8, transaminases normalized), the outside MRCP raised the possibility of cholangitis or autoimmune pancreatitis and recommended follow-up imaging. A bedside RUQ ultrasound is the logical first step at this institution — it is radiation-free (critical in pregnancy), rapidly available, and will directly assess the gallbladder, CBD caliber, and pericholecystic fluid to determine whether active cholecystitis or persistent biliary obstruction is present and guide further management.

representative_ex_ante.appropriateness_reason: The patient transferred with outside imaging showing CBD dilation (7.6 mm), gallbladder wall thickening, and biliary tree irregularities on MRCP, but no same-institution imaging exists; a repeat RUQ ultrasound is the appropriate first-line, radiation-free, pregnancy-safe study to establish a current baseline and guide management decisions.

representative_ex_ante.reasoning: This pregnant patient transferred with documented cholelithiasis, prior CBD dilation, gallbladder wall thickening, and biliary tree irregularities on outside imaging, but no current-institution imaging exists. Although her labs are trending toward normal (lipase 69, total bilirubin 0.8, transaminases normalized), the outside MRCP raised the possibility of cholangitis or autoimmune pancreatitis and recommended follow-up imaging. A bedside RUQ ultrasound is the logical first step at this institution — it is radiation-free (critical in pregnancy), rapidly available, and will directly assess the gallbladder, CBD caliber, and pericholecystic fluid to determine whether active cholecystitis or persistent biliary obstruction is present and guide further management.

vindication.actual_finding: Moderately distended gallbladder with wall thickening up to 5 mm, a proximally dilated common bile duct at 7 mm, sludge in the gallbladder neck, a positive sonographic Murphy sign, but no cholelithiasis or intrahepatic biliary dilatation.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The report directly confirmed the expected findings of gallbladder wall thickening and common bile duct dilation, consistent with ongoing cholecystitis or biliary pathology, though no discrete stones were identified (sludge was noted instead).

ensemble.other_hypothesis: choledocholithiasis / biliary duct obstruction; cholangitis; passed common bile duct stone with residual biliary tree abnormality
ensemble.information_gap: Whether a stone remains in the distal common bile duct or whether biliary tree abnormalities (narrowing/dilatation seen on prior MRCP) represent residual cholangitis vs. another etiology — the prior MRCP was performed at the outside hospital and was equivocal, and the current RUQ ultrasound again shows CBD dilatation (7 mm) without visualizing the distal duct or a stone, leaving the biliary anatomy incompletely characterized at this institution.
ensemble.expected_finding: Presence or absence of choledocholithiasis in the distal CBD, degree of intra- and extra-hepatic biliary dilatation, and evidence of active pancreatitis or cholangitis — essentially repeating/confirming the MRCP findings in-house without ionizing radiation given the pregnancy.
ensemble.appropriateness_reason: MRI abdomen without contrast is the appropriate next step in a pregnant patient with persistent CBD dilatation on ultrasound, equivocal outside MRCP, mildly elevated lipase, and prior recommendation for MRCP — it avoids ionizing radiation while providing superior biliary duct visualization compared to ultrasound.
grounding: History: 'She was seen by GI and general surg at ___, recommended MRCP'

Prior imaging (outside RUQ US ___): 'common bile duct dilated measuring 7.6mm... stone may be within the distal CBD, which was not visualized on this exam. An MRCP can be performed if clinically indicated'
Prior imaging (outside MRCP ___): 'A few areas of narrowing and dilatation of the intra- and extra-hepatic biliary tree... No evidence of choledocholithiasis... No MR evidence of pancreatitis'

Current RUQ US: 'The common bile duct is dilated proximally, measuring 7 mm, although it appears to taper down distally... There is no evidence of cholelithiasis identified... positive sonographic ___ sign'
Labs: 'Lipase: 69.0 IU/L (ref 0.0-60.0)' — mildly elevated
Labs (outside ___): 'T. bili 2.2 D. bili 1.7 Amylase 164 Lipase 488' — prior significant elevation now trending down
History: 'G1P0 at ___' — pregnant patient, radiation avoidance is a priority
ensemble.reasoning: This pregnant patient transferred specifically for MRCP evaluation of her biliary tree, which was recommended at the outside hospital after an equivocal RUQ ultrasound showing CBD dilatation without a visualized stone. The current in-house ultrasound again demonstrates CBD dilatation to 7 mm with a positive Murphy sign but no stone identified, and the outside MRCP was inconclusive (possible passed stone vs. cholangitis vs. congenital variation). Ordering an in-house MRI abdomen without contrast allows definitive characterization of the biliary anatomy and detection of any residual choledocholithiasis while avoiding ionizing radiation in this pregnant patient — directly addressing the outstanding diagnostic question that prompted the transfer.

vindication.actual_finding: MRCP showed no choledocholithiasis or obstructing mass, mild focal narrowing at the origins of both hepatic ducts with CBD/common hepatic duct dilatation to 9 mm and moderate narrowing at the ampulla, gallbladder edema with sludge/tiny stones, and a normal pancreas without edema or fluid collections.
vindication.relation_to_expectation: The test directly addressed the expected findings by characterizing the biliary anatomy in-house, ruling out a distal CBD stone, quantifying the degree of biliary dilatation, and confirming absence of active pancreatitis — resolving the key information gap from the prior equivocal outside MRCP.
